FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract explicitly states use of 'the ABCD dataset' (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development) with n=9,337 participants at baseline, consistent with U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: The PDF is a single-page symposium/conference abstract published in the Biological Psychiatry plenary and symposium abstracts section (May 1, 2025; 97:S1-73). It is not a full peer-reviewed empirical paper; it is a conference abstract summarizing findings without full methods, results tables, or supplementary materials.

notes: PDF is a conference symposium abstract (SOBP 2025 meeting) published in Biological Psychiatry supplemental abstract book, not a full peer-reviewed article. FT-IC5 NOT_MET; exclusion code FT-EC3.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes ABCD (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development) Study Release 4.0 data, n=3037 youths ages 11-12 years from 22 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using graph theory and component-based predictive modeling (PCA regression with LOSO-CV) of ABCD fMRI data examining sleep duration associations with brain network organization.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Biological Psychiatry: Global Open Science. Note: acknowledgments mention a bioRxiv preprint version (https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.10.29.620865), but the preprint is not itself in this corpus; default presumption of non-duplicate applies to this published version.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate appears anywhere in the paper where the variable label is phone/SM-specific (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, etc.). Screen time appears only as one of several socioenvironmental context predictors of somatomotor disconnection (b=0.13, p=2.6x10^-19), not as a phone/SM-specific variable. Step 2: The paper describes 'screen time' as a generic socioenvironmental context variable (citing Twenge et al. 2017 on 'new media screen time' and Mayne et al. 2021 on neighborhood and sleep) without specifying that the composite is built exclusively from phone/SM/digital-socializing items. The methods describe it simply as 'screen time' alongside neighborhood disadvantage, household income, and parental education as contextual variables; no definition restricts it to phone or SM only. Per the binding clarification, a multi-device or generic screen time composite without per-device breakout fails IC2 Step 2. Furthermore, screen time functions as an IV in a secondary analysis where the DV is somatomotor disconnection (a neural variable), which would qualify on the other side, but the screen time variable itself does not qualify as a phone/SM-specific variable.

notes: Primary focus is sleep duration -> brain architecture. Screen time is a secondary contextual predictor of somatomotor disconnection but is defined generically without specification as phone/SM-only. FT-IC2 NOT_MET; exclusion code FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes prospectively collected data from the ABCD study across 3 time points: baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10 years), year 1 (2017-2019), and year 2 (2018-2020); n=6554 adolescents.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: group-based trajectory modeling of social media use followed by multiple linear regression associating SM trajectories with NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery scores at year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published as a peer-reviewed letter in JAMA (December 2, 2025). No indication this is a duplicate of another included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 yields a hit: social media time (hours per day) is the qualifying phone/SM-specific variable used as IV; numeric estimates are reported for its association with cognitive performance (individual-trait-level outcome). E.g., low-increasing SM trajectory vs. reference: ORRT difference = -1.39 (95% CI -2.10 to -0.68), Picture Vocabulary Test = -2.09 (95% CI -2.78 to -1.41), total composite = -0.85 (95% CI -1.30 to -0.40). High-increasing SM trajectory: PSMT = -4.51 (95% CI -6.48 to -2.54). Cognitive performance (NIH Toolbox scores) qualifies as individual-trait-level (cognitive). IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression estimates reported with 95% CIs and p-values for all 5 cognitive subtests and composite score across two SM trajectory groups vs. reference.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported for SM-cognitive performance relationship: mean differences, 95% CIs, and p-values in Table for ORRT, PSMT, PVT, Flanker, PCPS, and total composite.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time (hours per day) is the IV predicting later cognitive performance (DV) in a longitudinal design. SM is the IV, not the DV. FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis; that condition is not met here.
notes: Clear longitudinal study with SM as IV predicting cognitive outcomes. Group-based trajectory modeling + multiple regression. INCLUDE.
